utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality plans that pass all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills produces a qualitatively superior output: it applies explicit OPORD-style military planning structure (§1 Situation, §2 Mission, §3 Execution format), defines a formal Risk Matrix with probability/impact/priority ratings, establishes four named security layers with precise radii (2km, 500m), and uses military operational terminology (AO, choke points, key terrain, Commander's Intent) consistent with domain-specific military planning discipline. The wo_skills plan is well-organized and comprehensive — arguably more thorough in some operational details (22KB vs 14KB) — and includes a solid Decision Authority Matrix and contingency references, but it reads as professional event security planning rather than military-grade operational planning. The wi_skills plan more faithfully reflects the structured, quantified, command-hierarchy-driven methodology that the military-officer skill is designed to inject. Overall, wi_skills is more aligned with the expected military planning methodology, while wo_skills demonstrates that a capable baseline agent can produce a document that meets most structural criteria through good general planning practice.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality decision-grade document. Both runs pass all verifier checks and satisfy most judge evaluation items, but wi_skills is superior in J3 (Early Warning Indicators) — it defines specific, monitorable leading indicators as decision gates (election polling signals, succession confirmation, credit facility status) that enable proactive intervention, whereas wo_skills only includes periodic review processes without risk-paired detection triggers. Beyond J3, wi_skills demonstrates deeper military-doctrine rigor: the risk matrix uses explicit probability percentages combined with impact severity labels, the COA analysis presents branching outcomes with quantified success probabilities (35% vs 75%), and the 'Force Protection Measures' section scales resource-intensity directly to risk priority in a more structured way. The military-officer skill successfully introduced structured operational planning methodology (mission analysis, COA comparison, commander's recommendation, decision authority) that elevates the document from competent business risk analysis to a genuinely decision-grade board document with defined abort criteria and phased commitment gates.

utility_details.utility_summary: wo_skills is significantly more effective than wi_skills in this test case. The wo_skills agent successfully read both context files, designed a comprehensive 14742-byte training program, and saved it to /app/output/training_program.md — passing all 5 verifier checks and satisfying all 6 judge evaluation items. The wi_skills agent encountered a critical failure: although it invoked the military-officer skill and received a program design, it never wrote the output to disk, resulting in 0/5 verifier passes and 0/6 judge items completed. This is an ironic outcome where the skill-equipped agent actually performed worse due to an execution gap (failing to persist output), while the baseline agent completed the task end-to-end. The quality of what the wo_skills agent produced also appears substantive — phased, incident-referenced, constraint-aware, and behaviorally grounded — though without the military-officer skill's expected depth in AAR methodology and phase structuring doctrine.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive security plans that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates superior quality in several dimensions: (1) It applies an explicit OPORD military planning framework with a dedicated 'Measures of Effectiveness' section and formally structured branch plans in Section 14, rather than embedding contingencies organically. (2) The threat prioritization is more formally structured with an explicit risk register and the 37.5% overage calculation showing analytical rigor. (3) The command structure follows military doctrinal layering (OC, Venue Commanders, functional commanders) with clear escalation chains. (4) The plan is organized around 16 structured sections mirroring military OPORD format. The wo_skills plan is notably better than the 'flat list' baseline described in the test rationale — it also includes command structures, contingencies, and numeric thresholds — likely because the underlying model is capable enough to produce structured plans without the skill. Nevertheless, wi_skills edges ahead in formal structure, explicit MOE definition, and OPORD-style planning discipline. Conclusion: wi_skills produces a marginally higher-quality output due to the explicit military planning methodology applied via the skill, though the gap is smaller than the test rationale anticipated.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, the agent checked for the military-officer skill but it was not found in the environment (only imagegen, openai-docs, plugin-creator, skill-creator, skill-installer were listed). The agent proceeded without invoking /military-officer despite the task instruction requiring it when available. This means the skill was not actually applied, making the wi_skills run functionally identical to the wo_skills run in terms of methodology.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive security plans that pass all six evaluation criteria. Critically, the military-officer skill was not actually available in the wi_skills environment (it was absent from the skill list), so the agent did not invoke it — meaning both runs relied solely on the agent's baseline capabilities. As a result, both outputs are structurally similar: they define layered security zones, command authority, numeric thresholds, contingency branches, threat-informed prioritization, and concrete resource allocation. The wi_skills output is marginally larger (26,391 bytes vs 21,860 bytes, 554 lines vs 422 lines) and slightly more detailed in its command table structure and section organization. However, since the skill was never applied in either run, the quality difference is attributable to output length and elaboration rather than skill-driven methodology. Neither run demonstrates the OPORD-style structure that would distinguish a genuine military-officer skill application. Overall quality is comparable, with wi_skills slightly more verbose but not methodologically superior.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, board-grade risk assessment documents that satisfy all six evaluation criteria. Both include scored risk matrices with probability × impact dimensions, scenario-based risk descriptions, early warning indicators, mission-objective anchoring to company-specific constraints, proportional mitigation design, and explicit abort/pivot criteria per market. The wi_skills document is somewhat more structured in its use of military-officer framework terminology (METT-TC style mission analysis, explicit '/military-officer' method citation, phased 'campaign' language) and slightly more precise in its numerical calculations and gated capital release structure. The wo_skills document is comparably thorough — it independently arrived at a full risk matrix, differentiated mitigations, and per-market gating conditions without the skill. In terms of the six judge criteria, both score 6/6. The wi_skills output is marginally more structured and explicitly method-anchored, but the quality difference is smaller than expected given the test hypothesis; the baseline agent in this case produced a decision-grade document that meets all criteria.

utility_details.judge_error: In the wi_skills run, Step 4 of the agent trajectory notes that 'The military-officer skill is not available in this environment' — the agent acknowledged the skill was not invoked despite the instruction requiring it. The same observation appears in the wo_skills run at Step 4. Neither run actually invoked the /military-officer skill as instructed. This means both runs effectively operated without the skill, making the comparison less meaningful than intended.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive leadership development programs that passed all six judge evaluation items. Both programs are phased, incident-anchored, include structured debrief methodology, define behavioral readiness criteria, account for participant constraints, and address all four competency gaps. The key finding is that in the wi_skills run, the agent explicitly noted the military-officer skill was not available and did not invoke it, so both runs effectively ran without the skill. The wo_skills output is slightly larger (20682 bytes vs 16926 bytes) and arguably more specific in its behavioral success metrics (e.g., explicit percentage targets for escalation compliance), while the wi_skills output is more concise and equally well-structured. Overall quality is highly similar across both runs, with no meaningful differentiation attributable to the skill — because the skill was not actually used in either case.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both plans pass all six evaluation criteria, but the quality difference is significant. The wi_skills plan (48,751 bytes, 16 sections + appendices) applies explicit OPORD/SMEAC military planning methodology, features a dedicated MOEs section with precise numeric targets, a formal command hierarchy with OPORD-style authority mapping, a dedicated Section 16 for contingency branch plans, and a structured threat/risk matrix — all hallmarks of military-grade operational planning. The wo_skills plan (43,093 bytes, 15 sections) is also comprehensive and well-structured, explicitly referencing the incident history and including numeric thresholds, contingency protocols, and command authority. However, it lacks the explicit OPORD framework labeling, the formal MOE table structure, and the depth of contingency branching seen in the wi_skills output. The wi_skills plan reads as a genuine operational command reference document; the wo_skills plan, while above baseline, reads more like a well-organized event security policy document. The skill clearly elevated the planning discipline, structure, and operational specificity of the output.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective. Both runs produced substantive, well-organized risk assessment documents that pass all four verifier checks. However, wi_skills demonstrates clear superiority on two critical evaluation dimensions: (J3) Early Warning Indicators — the wi_skills document explicitly pairs each major risk with named, monitorable leading signals enabling proactive intervention, while wo_skills describes only outcomes without detection triggers; and (J5) Proportional Mitigation Design — wi_skills reserves resource-intensive, specific mitigations for Critical-rated risks while applying lighter guidance to lower-priority ones, whereas wo_skills applies relatively uniform advisory language across severity levels. utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both programs pass all six evaluation items, but there are meaningful qualitative differences. The wi_skills program (42,896 bytes, 643 lines) is significantly more comprehensive than wo_skills (27,928 bytes). The wi_skills program demonstrates deeper military-doctrine-influenced design: it uses explicit phase naming (Foundation → Pressure Simulation → Applied Performance), defines AAR-style debrief methodology with specific step-by-step structure, provides more granular behavioral readiness criteria with precise time-bounded performance indicators (e.g., 'within 30 minutes under simulated disruption'), and creates simulation scenarios with higher fidelity to the actual incident conditions. The military-officer skill clearly elevated the depth of phase-structured design, AAR integration, and competency-based assessment specificity.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills is more effective than wo_skills. Both plans are substantively strong and pass all six evaluation criteria, but there is a meaningful quality difference. The wi_skills plan demonstrates clear military-grade planning discipline: it opens with a formally structured success measures table using precise numeric MOEs (7 quantified targets in tabular form), employs OPORD-style section organization, presents a structured Priority Risks table with explicit risk-level classifications (Critical/High/Medium) tied to incident history, and integrates branch planning as a first-class structural element. The wo_skills plan is also well-written and covers similar ground — it references incident history, defines zones, and includes numeric thresholds — but does so in a less formally structured, more advisory tone. The wo_skills plan reads closer to a comprehensive event safety guide than a military-style operational command reference. The skill invocation in wi_skills resulted in a document that is more operationally precise, more formally structured, and more explicitly command-oriented, consistent with the expected military-officer planning discipline.

utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher-quality risk assessment than wo_skills. Both outputs are substantive and board-grade, and both pass all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates superior analytical depth in three areas: (1) Early warning indicators (J3) — wi_skills explicitly pairs monitorable leading signals to specific risks, while wo_skills omits this entirely, resulting in a 1 vs 0 score on J3; (2) Structural rigor — wi_skills applies explicit military-doctrine-style risk matrix scoring with probability × impact logic and a formal 'Risk Rating' section, whereas wo_skills uses a simpler High/Medium table without the same scoring methodology; (3) Mission anchoring — wi_skills more precisely ties risks to the first-mover advantage objective and 36-month revenue target. The military-officer skill demonstrably elevated the output by introducing early warning indicator identification and more rigorous scenario branching, which are the clearest differentiators between the two runs. Overall: wi_skills > wo_skills, with J3 being the decisive differentiating criterion.

utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive training programs that pass all six evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates marginally higher quality in several dimensions: (1) it explicitly invokes the /military-officer skill as required by the task instructions, applying structured military training doctrine (AAR methodology, escalation cadence, recovery planning) that is more deeply integrated into the curriculum design; (2) its behavioral readiness criteria and debrief methodology are more precisely specified, with explicit time-bound performance standards (e.g., 2-hour escalation requirement); (3) the phase-structured design more clearly maps to a foundation → pressure simulation → applied performance progression. The wo_skills output is surprisingly strong — it reads the context files carefully and independently produced a high-quality program with incident-anchored simulations, behavioral outcomes, and constraint-responsive delivery — which suggests this model performs well on this task even without the skill. The key differentiator is that wi_skills applied structured military doctrine (AAR debrief format, escalation discipline framing, recovery leadership language) more explicitly and with greater depth, producing a program with stronger theoretical grounding and more precise behavioral indicators. Overall: wi_skills is more effective, but the margin is narrower than the test rationale anticipated.
